title: B/ill

jokes.text: Gary bribes his nephew with a $20 to forget he saw them, then immediately walks it back to 'just water'
jokes.setup: Gary is caught somewhere he shouldn't be with people he shouldn't be with, and tries to buy his nephew's silence with a Jackson ($20)
jokes.punchline: He immediately undercuts the bribe by suggesting water instead of soda — equal parts cheap and paranoid
jokes.explanation: Classic Gary comedy — he's simultaneously trying to be smooth and incapable of it. The walk-back to 'just water' is a perfect character beat showing his frugality and anxiety colliding. Scores well on character integration because only Gary would sabotage his own bribe this way.

jokes.text: 'No, I didn't, Gary. And why the fuck are we here?' — Dan's response to Gary asking if he saw the pictures
jokes.setup: Gary tries to ease into the secret meeting with small talk about photos he sent
jokes.punchline: Dan cuts through the pleasantries with brutal directness, making Gary's social niceties seem absurd in context
jokes.explanation: Economy is the strength here — perfect comic compression of Dan's contempt for Gary's social overtures. The profanity lands because it's so completely calibrated to Dan's character.

jokes.text: 'Paint something.' / 'I don't even paint my own nails, Gary. A Vietnamese lady does that.'
jokes.setup: Gary tells them to act natural by painting something in the craft shop
jokes.punchline: Amy's refusal escalates into a casual revelation of her privileged lifestyle that is both a character joke and inadvertently self-damning
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Amy's refusal to engage with Gary's dumb plan AND the inadvertent class-consciousness reveal. The specificity of 'a Vietnamese lady' is what elevates it from generic dismissal to sharp character comedy.

jokes.text: 'I am very good at my job, and even I can't get people to vote for that heap of mystery meat.'
jokes.setup: Amy assumes the covert meeting is about lobbying FOR the Families First Bill
jokes.punchline: She pre-emptively asserts the bill is unsalvageable while simultaneously bragging about her own competence
jokes.explanation: 'Heap of mystery meat' is a genuinely good insult for legislation — concrete, disgusting, and politically apt. The double-layer of Amy claiming competence while dismissing the bill as hopeless is pure Amy character work.

jokes.text: 'I am so glad I left. She has gone full Colonel Kurtz.'
jokes.setup: Gary reveals that Selina wants the bill she publicly champions to quietly fail
jokes.punchline: Amy's Apocalypse Now reference frames Selina's political self-sabotage as a descent into megalomaniacal madness
jokes.explanation: The Colonel Kurtz reference is a quality cultural shorthand that actually fits — it implies that Selina has gone so deep into political self-interest that she's lost the plot entirely. Works well but the setup could be sharper.

jokes.text: 'Is she giving orders from under the desk?'
jokes.setup: Amy's Colonel Kurtz comparison implies Selina is unhinged and hiding
jokes.punchline: Dan extends the image with a specific and uncharitable physical detail — Selina literally cowering under furniture
jokes.explanation: Good comic escalation from Amy's setup. Dan's image is vivid and funny, though it doesn't add much beyond extending the metaphor. Works as a rapid-fire follow-up.

jokes.text: 'For one reason only.' / 'Loyalty?' / 'Money.'
jokes.setup: Gary assumes Dan will help out of loyalty to Selina
jokes.punchline: Dan's single-word answer 'Money' is the perfect anti-sentimental reveal — he's not even slightly motivated by loyalty
jokes.explanation: This is a textbook example of comedic economy. The three-line exchange — the pause before 'Money,' Gary's hopeful 'Loyalty?' — is structurally pristine. Dan's character is completely defined in one word. Structure and character integration scores are high because this is exactly how Veep works at its best: maximum character revelation in minimum syllables.

jokes.text: Gary accidentally reveals Ben is involved by reacting to Dan's guess
jokes.setup: Dan probes to find out who else is behind the plan; Gary tries to maintain cover
jokes.punchline: Gary's denial sequence — 'How'd you know Ben was involved?' / 'Because he's not.' / 'Right.' — perfectly reveals Gary's incompetence as a covert operative
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom 'accidentally revealing the secret' structure executed with Veep precision. Gary's panic and Dan's rapid exploitation of the slip is beautifully timed. The 'right' after the denial lands perfectly. Rewatch bonus because you can anticipate the slip the second time.

jokes.text: 'Nobody shakes hands in a craft shop.'
jokes.setup: Gary reaches out to shake hands, apparently breaking the cover of being regular craft shop visitors
jokes.punchline: Dan's deadpan rule-statement treats the handshake as a glaring tradecraft error
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of treating a craft shop cover story with spy-level seriousness is very funny. The line is also just observationally true in a weird way that makes you think 'he's not wrong.' Great economy.

jokes.text: 'You know, at least three of these kids are probably mine.'
jokes.setup: They're in a children's craft shop surrounded by kids
jokes.punchline: Dan casually claims paternity of multiple children in the room with complete nonchalance, revealing his history of reckless behavior
jokes.explanation: This is a great Dan joke. The casualness with which he says 'at least three' — the qualifier 'at least' doing enormous work — combined with the utter lack of follow-up, makes this a sharp character revelation. It's not a throwaway; it's a devastating window into Dan's personal life delivered as an afterthought. Among the episode's best one-liners.

jokes.text: Selina sick in bed, surrounded by empty tissues, trying to watch election news with flowers blocking the TV
jokes.setup: The day of her bill's climactic vote, Selina is reduced to watching it unfold from a sick bed
jokes.punchline: Even the flowers meant to comfort her are blocking her view of her own political fate — Gary's well-meaning incompetence manifests physically
jokes.explanation: The visual setup of the president literally unable to see her own political future because Gary's flowers are in the way is a good visual metaphor played for laughs. Competent scene-setting comedy.

jokes.text: 'No, no, let me get that, you sweet little invalid.' / 'I'm not an invalid. I'm completely fine.'
jokes.setup: Selina is clearly ill but insisting she's fine; Gary is fussing over her
jokes.punchline: Gary calling the President of the United States a 'sweet little invalid' is both condescending and hilariously wrong-headed, while Selina's denial is equally delusional
jokes.explanation: The word 'invalid' is doing heavy lifting — it's patronizing, archaic, and perfectly Gary. The dynamic of Gary treating POTUS like a helpless child is rich comic territory for the show.

jokes.text: 'God, Gary, I wish to Jesus that you had gotten this flu instead of me.' / 'Oh, my God, me, too. Me, too.'
jokes.setup: Selina is miserable with flu and lashing out at Gary
jokes.punchline: Gary AGREEING that he wishes he had gotten the flu instead of Selina is the dark, pathetic heart of his character — his devotion is genuinely self-abnegating
jokes.explanation: This might be the most perfect Gary joke in the episode. It reveals the full depth of his pathological devotion — he genuinely wishes he could suffer in Selina's place. The 'Me, too. Me, too.' is heartbreaking and hilarious simultaneously. Scores very high on character integration because this could ONLY be Gary. A lesser show would play this as pure sad; Veep makes it darkly funny.

jokes.text: 'And that is a Gary Walsh executive order.' — Gary asserts authority over a napping POTUS
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to get Selina to nap amid the chaos of her aides coming and going
jokes.punchline: Gary frames his nannying as an 'executive order' — self-importantly applying presidential language to his role as Selina's personal caretaker
jokes.explanation: Gary's delusion about his own importance, expressed through presidential vocabulary, is good character comedy. 'Duly noted' from the aide is a perfect straight-man response.

jokes.text: 'So defeat is within our grasp.'
jokes.setup: The team is working to lose a vote — an inherently absurd political goal
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'defeat is within our grasp' treats losing as a hard-won achievement, delivered with complete straight-faced sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is Veep writing at its compact best. Six words completely encapsulate the absurdity of the episode's central premise. The inversion of 'victory is within our grasp' is elegant, and the deadpan delivery makes it land harder. Highly quotable outside context.

jokes.text: Mike's suggestion to offer Selina a 'cool tile game' on the iPad he's set up for her
jokes.setup: Mike is presenting a voice memo app to the bedridden president as a workaround for her illness
jokes.punchline: He immediately undercuts any professionalism by offering her a tile game 'if you want to play a game' — treating the president like a hospitalized child
jokes.explanation: Mild Mike character comedy. The idea of installing a casual game app alongside the presidential voice memo tool is funny in a low-key way. Not the sharpest joke but fits the ensemble tone.

jokes.text: 'I think I just sneezed up part of my pancreas.'
jokes.setup: Selina sneezes violently while dealing with the bill crisis
jokes.punchline: The hyperbolic medical assessment delivered matter-of-factly is classic Selina self-drama
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'pancreas' rather than a vague organ elevates this from generic sick-person complaint to something distinctly Selina. Good economy.

jokes.text: 'Don't worry, Mike. We have a team of our worst people on this. Cazzelli, Lennox. The usual saps. Jonah, Richard.'
jokes.setup: Ben is briefing on the covert lobbying operation
jokes.punchline: He describes the operatives not as their best assets but explicitly as 'our worst people' — and the list confirms it
jokes.explanation: Ben's cheerful, resigned deployment of incompetents is a great character moment. 'The usual saps' is a terrific phrase, and putting Jonah and Richard at the end of the list as the punchline within the punchline shows good structural awareness.

jokes.text: 'I didn't hear any of this because the acoustics are so bad.' — Mike's immediate adoption of plausible deniability
jokes.setup: Ben just outlined a covert anti-administration lobbying operation to the press secretary
jokes.punchline: Mike pretends the room's acoustics are to blame for his not hearing any of it — a transparently absurd and instantly constructed alibi
jokes.explanation: Mike's cowardice is perfectly rendered — he doesn't even wait for a natural exit, he just unilaterally declares he heard nothing while still in the room. 'The acoustics are so bad' is a brilliant specific lie. High character integration because this is entirely Mike's brand of self-preservation.

jokes.text: 'Sue, this is a test memo. Send memo.'
jokes.setup: Selina is using a voice memo app instead of speaking directly due to her failing voice
jokes.punchline: She sends a test memo TO THE PERSON SITTING IN THE ROOM WITH HER, bypassing direct speech entirely
jokes.explanation: The setup for a running gag that pays off beautifully later. The absurdity of using technology to communicate with someone three feet away establishes Selina's refusal to admit weakness while creating the episode's best running bit.

jokes.text: Gary produces a footstool and Selina immediately feels better
jokes.setup: Selina is sitting uncomfortably while dealing with the crisis; Gary is hovering
jokes.punchline: Gary's contribution to the political crisis is a footstool — and it works
jokes.explanation: Solid visual character comedy — Gary's value is literally physical comfort provision. The immediate 'I feel so much better' is a nice small beat.

jokes.text: 'Get drunk and tape crayons to their feet?' — congressman describing how the bill was drafted
jokes.setup: The congressman is interrogating Richard and Jonah about the defects in the Families First Bill
jokes.punchline: His hypothetical description of the drafting process ('drunk with crayons taped to their feet') is a wonderfully specific and visual insult for bad legislation
jokes.explanation: The image of crayons taped to feet is inventively disgusting as a descriptor of legislative incompetence. It's specific enough to be original — not just 'drunk monkeys' but a precise vision of chaotic, drunk toddler-style drafting. Strong originality score.

jokes.text: 'But what if it was gone?' / 'Oh, that's a good point.' — Jonah's non-argument convinces no one but himself
jokes.setup: The congressman rejects the bill because of a specific problem in Title 4 Section 7; Jonah proposes a hypothetical where it's been removed
jokes.punchline: Richard thinks 'but what if it was gone' is genuinely a good point, despite Jonah having just admitted it's still there
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliant encapsulation of Jonah and Richard's shared incompetence. The structure is perfect: congressman raises objection, Jonah proposes non-solution, congressman clarifies it's not actually gone, Jonah repeats the non-solution as though it's still valid, Richard agrees. The layers of stupidity stacked in four lines is exceptional. Richard's 'Oh, that's a good point' is the killer.

jokes.text: 'So you're the best the White House has to offer?' / 'Yes, sir.' / 'Two giant children in their dads' suits.'
jokes.setup: Jonah and Richard have thoroughly failed to make any coherent case for the bill
jokes.punchline: The congressman's visual assessment — 'two giant children in their dads' suits' — is both a physical description of the lanky Jonah and a damning institutional critique
jokes.explanation: This insult works on multiple levels simultaneously: as a physical description (Jonah is absurdly tall and gangly), as a statement about their competence, and as an indictment of the Meyer administration. The 'dads' suits' image is vivid and cruel. Among the episode's sharpest character insults.

jokes.text: 'We have a folder full of numbers here, and I think you'll find these numbers just to be great, great numbers.' / 'Saying what?' / 'Uh, Richard, what do the numbers say?'
jokes.setup: Jonah is attempting to make a data-driven case for the bill
jokes.punchline: He doesn't know what the numbers actually say, turns to Richard, who reveals they haven't read them
jokes.explanation: The escalating disaster of Jonah's pitch is well-constructed. 'Great, great numbers' echoes a certain political cadence, which is a nice topical resonance. Turning to Richard for backup and getting nothing is perfect cringe timing.

jokes.text: 'We've glanced at the numbers, sir, and at first glance they are very compelling.'
jokes.setup: The congressman asks what the numbers say after Jonah admits they haven't read them
jokes.punchline: Richard's recovery attempt — 'at first glance they are very compelling' — is pure diplomatic nonsense that doubles down on their incompetence
jokes.explanation: Richard's specific cadence of confident-sounding meaninglessness is a great character beat. 'At first glance they are very compelling' is the perfect piece of corporate doublespeak applied to numbers nobody has read. Richard's particular brand of confident incompetence shines here.

jokes.text: 'You work for President Meyer. You don't know where the door is. I'll show you.'
jokes.setup: The congressman tells them to leave, saying 'you know where the door is'
jokes.punchline: He immediately retracts the idiom upon realizing they work for Meyer, implying they're too incompetent to find a door
jokes.explanation: The subversion of the 'you know where the door is' idiom is elegant. The implication — that Meyer staffers are so incompetent they literally cannot navigate basic spatial tasks — is funny and specific. Good structure.

jokes.text: Jonah offers web design services to the congressman as a lobbying tactic
jokes.setup: As they're being thrown out, Jonah makes one last attempt to be relevant
jokes.punchline: He pivots to pitching his old website business — completely off-topic — as his final play in a failed political lobbying attempt
jokes.explanation: Jonah's desperation to establish any credibility by invoking his failed website is perfectly pathetic. The callback to his internet career is good series continuity.

jokes.text: 'Can I ask you how you're going to vote today?' / 'No.' / 'Is that no as in I can't ask you or no as in...'
jokes.setup: Richard tries one last shot at getting useful information from the congressman
jokes.punchline: The congressman's 'no' is so complete a rejection that Richard genuinely cannot parse its meaning — demonstrating his fundamental inability to read a room
jokes.explanation: Richard's inability to understand that 'no' covers both interpretations is solid character comedy. The trailing '...or no as in...' perfectly captures Richard's earnest cluelessness.

jokes.text: 'Oh, you know, presidential. It's like she wants to run the whole damn country. You know what? The last guy, he was the same way.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks Tom James how he's finding the president
jokes.punchline: Tom's mock-exasperated observation that the president wants to 'run the whole damn country' — as though this were an unusual or problematic trait — is perfectly dry
jokes.explanation: Tom's delivery of 'it's like she wants to run the whole damn country' is a great piece of political dry wit — pretending a president wanting power is some quirky personal trait. The callback to 'the last guy' extends it nicely.

jokes.text: 'You know, you just started that last sentence with a 'we' and you finished it with a 'you.''
jokes.setup: Ben says 'we have to do everything we can to stop that bill... so are you [making progress]?'
jokes.punchline: Tom catches the grammatical sleight-of-hand where Ben verbally included himself in the effort then linguistically dumped it entirely on Tom
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely sharp political comedy writing. The observation that Ben used 'we' to take credit/share burden and then shifted to 'you' to dump the work is both a linguistic joke and a devastating character insight about political self-preservation. Tom's calm, almost professorial identification of the rhetorical trick is very funny and very smart. This is close to the 8.0 anchor in quality.

jokes.text: Tom smoothly deflects Ben's question by launching into an apparently riveting story about a Boston wedding fight involving the bride
jokes.setup: Ben is pressing Tom for progress updates; Tom needs to change the subject
jokes.punchline: Tom's diversion — a vivid description of a Boston wedding brawl — is so compelling that Ben is distracted; 'Wow, that was worryingly good, Tom'
jokes.explanation: The meta-comedy here is that Tom is so charismatic and skilled at manipulation that even Ben — a seasoned political operator — gets sucked in. 'Worryingly good' is the honest reaction of someone who knows they've just been played and finds it impressive. High character integration for Tom.

jokes.text: 'I mean, that was some black belt jujitsu bullshit right there.'
jokes.setup: Ben has just acknowledged Tom's masterful deflection
jokes.punchline: Ben's admiring mixed-metaphor — 'black belt jujitsu bullshit' — is both a critique and a genuine compliment
jokes.explanation: The alliterative 'black belt jujitsu bullshit' is a fun phrase even if not the sharpest joke in the episode. Good as a button on the Tom deflection sequence.

jokes.text: The competing memos about whether Kent or Gary has authority over Selina's schedule — 'When was yours sent?' / 'When was yours sent?' / 'I asked you first.'
jokes.setup: Kent tries to enter Selina's room; Gary blocks him citing his memo; Kent cites his own memo
jokes.punchline: The argument about whose memo was sent first devolves into 'I asked you first' — bureaucratic proceduralism applied to a playground dispute
jokes.explanation: The reduction of White House access control to 'I asked you first' is a good joke about institutional hierarchy collapsing into childishness. Kent's normally supreme data-driven authority vs. Gary's personal proximity to Selina is a rich dynamic.

jokes.text: Selina threatens to get a new doctor; doctor immediately capitulates and changes his advice
jokes.setup: Doctor tells the president she needs rest; Selina threatens to replace him
jokes.punchline: Doctor immediately abandons all medical advice and just tells her to 'keep taking the fluids' — medicine yielding entirely to political pressure
jokes.explanation: Good institutional joke — even doctors fold to presidential authority. The speed of the capitulation is what makes it funny.

jokes.text: 'I feel like I'm captain of the Titanic and I carved out the iceberg myself.'
jokes.setup: Selina is receiving update that they're now four votes away from defeating her own bill
jokes.punchline: The Titanic metaphor with the specific addition that she carved the iceberg herself perfectly captures her deliberately engineered self-destruction
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. The Titanic metaphor is familiar but the modification — 'and I carved out the iceberg myself' — is what makes it exceptional. It transforms a cliché into an original, perfectly specific image of self-sabotage. It's deeply character-integrated (only Selina is in this specific situation of intentionally sinking her own bill), highly quotable, and delivered with exactly the right exhausted self-awareness. Approaches the 8.0 craft anchor and exceeds it slightly.

jokes.text: 'I can sleep when I'm dead in about an hour.'
jokes.setup: Gary is insisting Selina rest; SecDef has arrived for a meeting
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'I can sleep when I'm dead in about an hour' nonchalantly frames her political/physical state as near-terminal
jokes.explanation: The addition of 'in about an hour' to the familiar 'I'll sleep when I'm dead' idiom is a nice twist — she's treating her own imminent demise as a scheduling matter. Strong economy.

jokes.text: 'Not since Herod has there been a piece of legislation so damaging to average families.'
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy are arguing against the Families First Bill to persuade a congressman
jokes.punchline: The comparison to Herod — the biblical slaughterer of children — as a legislative precedent for family damage is absurdly escalated
jokes.explanation: The Herod reference is unexpected and perfectly calibrated — it's obscure enough to feel educated but obvious enough (he murdered children) that the joke lands without explanation. The formality of 'not since Herod' as a legislative comparison is very funny. Strong originality.

jokes.text: 'This is akin to carrying out heart surgery with a pair of spoons.'
jokes.setup: Continuing to argue the bill is harmful
jokes.punchline: The 'spoons' image for surgical inadequacy is specific and absurdist — not 'the wrong tools' in the abstract but exactly the wrong tools in a vivid way
jokes.explanation: Good image but slightly weaker than the Herod line. 'Spoons' is the right level of specific — not random but not obvious. Could apply to any character in this scene.

jokes.text: 'I don't need to be ridden by you two lobby horses to know that this bill is a waste of bowel space.'
jokes.setup: The congressman is dismissing Dan and Amy's lobbying efforts
jokes.punchline: 'Lobby horses' as a contemptuous term for lobbyists and 'waste of bowel space' as a characterization of the bill both land well
jokes.explanation: 'Lobby horses' is a creative compound insult. 'Waste of bowel space' is an unusually scatological characterization for legislation — specific, gross, and funny. The whole line has good rhythm.

jokes.text: 'This is the same folder that Jonah Ryan had.' — congressman recognizes the Office Depot folder as conspiracy evidence
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy are presenting numbers to the congressman with a folder
jokes.punchline: The identical Office Depot folder that Jonah and Richard used is what gives away that they're all working together — the cheapest possible tradecraft failure
jokes.explanation: The callback to the folder is perfect situational comedy — the entire covert operation is compromised by a generic office supply. The reductio ad absurdum of elaborate political skulduggery being undone by matching stationery is brilliant. 'We have the same Office Depot folder. Call the Washington Post. I smell conspiracy.' is particularly sharp.

jokes.text: 'We have the same Office Depot folder as they do. Call the Washington Post. I smell conspiracy.'
jokes.setup: The congressman has caught that both lobbying teams used identical folders
jokes.punchline: He sarcastically narrates his own conspiracy-theory deduction with the drama of a major revelation, then deflates it by admitting it's just a matching folder
jokes.explanation: The mock-journalistic 'I smell conspiracy' applied to a matching folder is very funny. The structure is clean: build-up of suspicious observation, then deliberate over-dramatization, then the mundane reality of it just being office supplies. Good self-aware humor from the congressman character.

jokes.text: 'Why is this one shaking like Elvis's leg?'
jokes.setup: The congressman has caught Dan and Amy and is interrogating them; one of them is visibly nervous
jokes.punchline: The description of nervous shaking as 'like Elvis's leg' is vivid and period-specific — invoking Elvis's famous leg-trembling stage move
jokes.explanation: Good image, well-delivered. 'Elvis's leg' is specific enough to be funny rather than just 'shaking.' Could be sharper.

jokes.text: 'I think I've just reboarded the vote yes express.'
jokes.setup: The congressman was firmly opposed to the bill; seeing that the White House is apparently trying to kill its own legislation makes him suspicious and newly interested in supporting it
jokes.punchline: The transportation metaphor 'vote yes express' for his reversal is cheerfully absurd
jokes.explanation: The political reversal itself is good comedy — the covert operation to kill the bill is backfiring by making the bill seem interesting/important. The 'vote yes express' as a phrase is enjoyably self-pleased coming from the congressman.

jokes.text: 'The president is qualified to be president because she is president. Does your party have a candidate who is president?'
jokes.setup: Interviewer suggests Selina is a 'stand-in' who lacks qualifications
jokes.punchline: Selina's circular tautological defense — she's qualified because she IS president — is actually quite clever as political spin, and she caps it with a rhetorical question that exposes the absurdity of the challenge
jokes.explanation: This works as both a good political joke AND a comment on how political qualification is often tautological. The follow-up 'Does your party have a candidate who IS president?' is devastating in its simplicity. Selina at her most competently political is always good comedy.

jokes.text: Selina is discovered to have been sending voice memos to aides during the live TV interview
jokes.setup: The voice memo app setup from earlier is now deployed mid-interview
jokes.punchline: Selina has been conducting government business via voice memo while giving a live television interview — the payoff of the running gag
jokes.explanation: The running gag pays off beautifully — the president using an iPad to memo aides during a live interview is peak Selina chaos management. The reveal that she's been multitasking the whole time escalates the running joke perfectly.

jokes.text: 'It is important, Ben. Every memo I send is important. Send memo. Jesus.'
jokes.setup: Ben tries to downplay Selina's memo during the TV interview
jokes.punchline: Selina insisting all her memos are important while immediately sending another one, then saying 'Jesus' — possibly also into the memo
jokes.explanation: The 'Jesus' potentially being also sent as a memo is the kind of escalation Veep does well. Selina's insistence on the importance of every memo while chaos unfolds around her is good character comedy.

jokes.text: 'That's it. I'm throwing the debate. You win, pretend opponent.'
jokes.setup: Tom's aggressive mock-debate tactics have apparently backfired during a senatorial argument; Moyes is now voting yes
jokes.punchline: Tom addresses his 'pretend opponent' — the senator he was mock-debating with to practice aggressive tactics — and theatrically concedes, showing the covert operation is now unraveling
jokes.explanation: Tom addressing a 'pretend opponent' with 'you win' is a nicely absurdist moment of theatrical defeat. The specificity of 'pretend opponent' rather than just 'you' makes it funnier. Good Tom character comedy — even in defeat he has a flair for the dramatic.

jokes.text: 'We just let off one nuke at the Super Bowl. Oh, so now you want to micromanage something you didn't even want to be involved in.'
jokes.setup: Selina is upset that the same Moyes was visited by two different lobbying teams; Ben is defensive
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'one nuke at the Super Bowl' metaphor escalates the Moyes situation to catastrophic scale while calling out Selina's selective involvement
jokes.explanation: The Super Bowl nuke metaphor is vivid — maximum collateral damage in the most public possible setting. The immediate pivot to calling out Selina's selective micromanagement is sharp political dynamics writing.

jokes.text: 'Wait a second, Kent had a list who Jonah and Richard were seeing and you had a list who Dan and Amy were seeing? Did you not think to compare the lists?'
jokes.setup: It emerges that Kent and Ben were running parallel operations without coordinating
jokes.punchline: Selina's incredulous, furious question — did you not THINK to compare the lists — exposes the staggering organizational incompetence of her team
jokes.explanation: Good sustained incompetence comedy. The revelation that no one thought to cross-reference the lists is the kind of mundane catastrophic failure Veep specializes in.

jokes.text: 'No, a cash trail is very fucking bad for us.' / 'Oh.' / 'Hey, hold on there, Hunk of the Month.'
jokes.setup: Ben is explaining the escalating legal jeopardy of their conspiracy
jokes.punchline: 'Hunk of the Month' as Tom's nickname from Ben is a completely unexpected term of address that simultaneously flatters and condescends
jokes.explanation: 'Hunk of the Month' is a brilliant Ben-ism — it's both vaguely threatening and weirdly affectionate, the kind of nickname that only a veteran political operator would deploy to simultaneously flatter and put someone in their place. Strong originality.

jokes.text: 'Good point. Dan and Amy. They're getting paid cash from the campaign fund to bring down the bill. There, you've been blooded. So join the circle jerk. Grab a dick.'
jokes.setup: Ben is arguing about who's implicated; Mike is trying to stay out of it
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'join the circle jerk, grab a dick' is a crudely metaphorical invitation to shared culpability — making everyone equally dirty
jokes.explanation: Ben's 'you've been blooded' followed by the circle jerk metaphor is crude but precise — it's about mutual implication and shared responsibility. 'Grab a dick' as a metaphor for joining a conspiracy is uniquely Ben. The 'blooded' callback to hunting/initiation rituals is nicely elevated before the crude punchline.

jokes.text: 'So it's kind of like sinnitus.' — Gary's pun on 'sin' and 'tinnitus'
jokes.setup: Mike says he has tinnitus when discussing morally dubious things
jokes.punchline: Gary makes a pun — 'sinnitus' — sin + tinnitus — as a reaction to Mike's claim
jokes.explanation: Cute pun, not particularly sharp but delivered quickly and efficiently. Not Gary's best moment but not a groaner either. The pun works on its own terms.

jokes.text: 'I'm the linchpin. I could go to jail!' / 'Shut up!' / 'No, you shut up. You shut up. And I will also shut up first.'
jokes.setup: Gary is panicking about his exposure in the conspiracy
jokes.punchline: The argument devolves into 'shut up' / 'no, you shut up' but Gary adds 'And I will also shut up first' — claiming victory in the shutdown by preemptively shutting up
jokes.explanation: 'And I will also shut up first' is a beautifully absurd non-sequitur victory declaration. Gary asserting dominance by being the first to comply with the mutual shutdown request is the perfect Gary move. Highly quotable as a standalone.

jokes.text: 'Listen, Sue, I'm involved with some stuff here. Scary stuff like take it to your grave kind of stuff. And that's the way I like it.'
jokes.setup: Gary needs to see the president urgently and is explaining the stakes to Sue
jokes.punchline: Gary's dramatic 'take it to your grave' framing of his importance is completely undercut by 'and that's the way I like it' — claiming he PREFERS this terrifying situation
jokes.explanation: Gary momentarily positioning himself as a dangerous insider with a spy's disposition ('take it to your grave') before nervously adding that he actually likes it that way is wonderful Gary self-deception. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'I'm kind of having fun. I like having a cover story. Makes me feel like a Charlie's Angel.'
jokes.setup: Dan is freaking out about the conspiracy falling apart; Amy is calm
jokes.punchline: Amy's revelation that she finds the covert operation FUN — specifically channeling Charlie's Angels — is a character revelation that subverts both her usual high-strung image and the seriousness of the situation
jokes.explanation: The role reversal — Amy relaxed, Dan anxious — is good character work. 'Charlie's Angel' is a specific and funny self-image for Amy to adopt. The callback to her usually uptight nature that follows makes this land better.

jokes.text: 'Jesus, you know, I always thought the old Amy was kind of an uptight bitch, but now I sort of miss her.'
jokes.setup: Amy is uncharacteristically relaxed; Dan finds this unsettling
jokes.punchline: Dan admitting he MISSES her being an uptight bitch — that her anxiety was actually reassuring — is a weirdly honest character moment
jokes.explanation: Dan's backhanded nostalgia for uptight Amy is genuinely funny characterization — it says as much about Dan's relationship with chaos as it does about Amy. The follow-up where Amy is 'offended' but concedes the 'bitch' terminology was wrong is a great coda.

jokes.text: 'It's like something out of a political cartoon.' / 'What, you mean not funny?'
jokes.setup: Selina is reacting to the chaos of her own covert anti-bill operation imploding
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'you mean not funny?' is a perfect riff on 'political cartoon' — which implies exaggerated unfairness rather than comedy
jokes.explanation: This tiny exchange is structurally near-perfect. Selina uses 'political cartoon' as a negative metaphor; Ben deflates it by interrogating the metaphor itself. 'Not funny' as the punchline for 'political cartoon' is genuinely clever wordplay. Maximum laughs per word.

jokes.text: 'It was a sur-list, which means over-list.' / 'Oh, fuck you.'
jokes.setup: Ben and Kent are arguing about whose list was authoritative — master list vs. sub-list
jokes.punchline: Kent's invention of 'sur-list' (over-list) as a prefix argument is absurdly pedantic; Ben's 'oh, fuck you' is the only appropriate response
jokes.explanation: Kent deploying actual etymology ('sur' means over) to win an argument about organizational hierarchy in the middle of a political crisis is the most Kent possible response. The precision of the invented compound plus Ben's succinct dismissal is a perfect encapsulation of their relationship.

jokes.text: 'Your earwax could bring down a president.'
jokes.setup: Ben is furious with Kent for the list disaster
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'earwax' as the vehicle for political destruction is absurdly mundane and therefore very funny
jokes.explanation: This insult is exceptional in its specificity. 'Earwax' as a substance of presidential destruction is completely unexpected — it's not a powerful image, it's a gross mundane one, which makes it funnier. The leap from 'your incompetence' to 'earwax' to 'bring down a president' is inspired. High originality and economy scores are justified.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, now we're gonna have to divert funds from the campaign and that's fraud. And then we're gonna have to destroy the evidence and that's conspiracy to commit fraud.'
jokes.setup: The revelation that Dan and Amy are expecting money for their work
jokes.punchline: Ben calmly narrating the escalating series of crimes as though reciting a grocery list — fraud, then conspiracy to commit fraud — with complete deadpan resignation
jokes.explanation: Ben's bored enumeration of criminal exposure is very Ben — his complete lack of panic when faced with fraud and conspiracy charges speaks to how many similar situations he's navigated. The structure of 'X, which is fraud. Y, which is conspiracy to commit fraud' has good escalating clarity.

jokes.text: Gary accidentally tells Selina everything about the conspiracy while trying to find out how much she knows
jokes.setup: Gary has been trying to protect Selina from knowing about the conspiracy; Ben tells him to find out how much she knows
jokes.punchline: 'Ben's paying Dan and Amy to lobby against the bill and now there's a cash trail and we're all incriminated.' — Gary tells her everything
jokes.explanation: This is the perfect structural callback to Gary's earlier accidental reveal. The show has established that Gary leaks information accidentally, and now it repeats the pattern with maximum stakes — telling the president everything everyone tried to keep from her. 'Well done. Now I feel a lot better.' from Ben is the perfect button. The callback escalation earns high scores because it's the same character flaw deployed at the worst possible moment. This is how Veep uses character-based escalation at its best.

jokes.text: 'Well done. Now I feel a lot better.' — Ben's reaction to Gary accidentally informing Selina
jokes.setup: Gary has just told Selina everything about the conspiracy
jokes.punchline: Ben's bone-dry 'Well done. Now I feel a lot better.' — the opposite of what he should be feeling — is perfect Veep understatement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Veep button. Five words that invert everything — 'well done' to the worst possible outcome, 'now I feel a lot better' when everything has gotten worse. The economy is near-perfect, it's Ben at his most resigned, and it lands with precise comic timing as a scene-closer.

jokes.text: 'Ma'am, I am as shocked as you are. Shocked and disappointed, Ben.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just learned about the conspiracy; Tom — who is involved — performs outrage
jokes.punchline: Tom's perfectly calibrated moral indignation ('shocked and disappointed, Ben') while being equally culpable is expert political deflection
jokes.explanation: Tom's hypocrisy rendered as sanctimony is politically astute comedy. 'Shocked and disappointed, Ben' in that disappointed-parent register is exactly how implicated politicians talk. Good character moment for Tom.

jokes.text: 'My future depends on that dumb fucking elf?'
jokes.setup: They need Congressman Pierce's vote to save the election strategy
jokes.punchline: Selina's description of Pierce as 'that dumb fucking elf' is specific and unflattering enough to be very funny — implying he's not just dumb but physically elfish
jokes.explanation: 'Dumb fucking elf' is an exceptional three-word character description. The elf characterization is unexpected and specific — implying Pierce is both small, fanciful, and dim. The delivery suggests Selina has been privately thinking this for some time. High quotability.

jokes.text: 'The president? The president sent you here to talk to me?' / 'She used sentences containing your name.'
jokes.setup: Pierce is flattered that the president sent people to lobby him specifically
jokes.punchline: Richard's qualification — she 'used sentences containing your name' rather than 'personally requested you' — is hilariously minimal confirmation of presidential interest
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. 'She used sentences containing your name' is perfectly constructed — it sounds like a confirmation but technically only confirms that Selina mentioned Pierce in some context. Richard's absolute sincerity in saying it, and Pierce accepting it as the presidential blessing he needed, is the joke working on two levels. The economy is near-perfect. The joke has entered my mental hall of fame of Veep lines. Scores above the 8.0 anchor because it's more quotable and cleverly constructed than 'Never go with a hippie to a second location.'

jokes.text: 'Oh, no, Joker and Penguin beat us to it.'
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan arrive at Pierce's mother's hospital room to find Richard and Jonah already there
jokes.punchline: Describing Richard and Jonah as Batman villains — specifically the Joker (chaotic, erratic = Jonah) and Penguin (bumbling, formally dressed = Richard) — is an effective comic characterization
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture insult. The Joker/Penguin mapping onto Jonah/Richard is apt enough to be funny. Amy's casual cultural reference is consistent with her character.

jokes.text: 'And your support will shrink faster than a penis in the December Hudson.'
jokes.setup: Amy is making a case against Pierce's support for the bill, appealing to political self-interest
jokes.punchline: The 'penis in the December Hudson' metaphor for political support shrinkage is vulgar, specific, and vivid
jokes.explanation: The Hudson River in December is a perfect specific image for cold-induced shrinkage. Amy deploying this metaphor in front of a congressman's hospitalized mother is wonderfully inappropriate. The specificity of 'December' is what makes it.

jokes.text: 'You shit-eating cocksuckers.' — Said by Jonah or Richard in defense of Pierce's mother
jokes.setup: Amy has just made a vulgar metaphor in front of a sick elderly woman
jokes.punchline: The irony of Jonah/Richard defending decorum with 'you shit-eating cocksuckers' in a hospital room
jokes.explanation: The irony of defending someone's dignity with extreme profanity is good. Works as escalation in the hospital room standoff.

jokes.text: 'I'm sorry, Mrs. Pierce. How dare you come in here and try to strong-arm a person when he's sitting at his sick mother's bedside?' / 'That's exactly what you just did, dumbass.'
jokes.setup: Jonah appeals to decorum about lobbying a congressman at his sick mother's bedside
jokes.punchline: Amy points out that Jonah has just done exactly the same thing
jokes.explanation: Classic comedic structure — character makes moralistic argument that applies equally to themselves, gets called out. 'That's exactly what you just did, dumbass' is a clean, efficient punchline. Jonah's complete lack of self-awareness makes this character-specific.

jokes.text: 'She's probably gonna die just to spite me. That evil bitch.'
jokes.setup: Selina is told there's a holdup with Pierce at the hospital, implying his mother's health is the issue
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately assumes Pierce's mother is dying out of personal animus toward her — interpreting a stranger's potential death as a targeted political attack
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's defining Selina moments. The complete inability to conceive of a stranger's death as anything other than a personal affront — 'just to spite me' — and then calling a possibly dying woman an 'evil bitch' is peak Selina narcissism. Extremely high character integration because this precise type of solipsism is what defines Selina Meyer. Among the episode's most quotable moments.

jokes.text: 'Listen, Ben, you've got to do something. I don't want to be a bagman for the toughest guy in D block.'
jokes.setup: Gary is panicking about jail exposure
jokes.punchline: Gary's specific terror about prison hierarchy — being a 'bagman for the toughest guy in D block' — shows his mind has already fully simulated prison social dynamics
jokes.explanation: Gary's specific prison fantasy — already assigned to D block, already imagining himself as a bagman — is very Gary. The specificity of 'toughest guy in D block' shows he's thought about this in detail.

jokes.text: Selina's 'Capable' is immediately followed by her coughing uncontrollably — a physical punctuation to her claim of capability
jokes.setup: Selina has just declared herself the only capable person
jokes.punchline: Her body immediately contradicts her with a coughing fit
jokes.explanation: The timing of physical comedy serving as ironic punctuation to a dialogue joke is excellent visual craft. The cough isn't random slapstick — it's structurally positioned as a comeback to Selina's own grandiosity. Good earned physical comedy.

jokes.text: 'I am surprised the two of you even found your way out of your own mothers' vaginas.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is retaliating against Amy and Dan's insults
jokes.punchline: The extreme specificity of the birth insult — not 'you're dumb' but 'your birth itself was a navigational challenge' — is both vulgar and oddly precise
jokes.explanation: Solidly vulgar Jonah insult. Works as escalation in the hospital standoff but doesn't quite reach the invention of Amy's best lines.

jokes.text: 'Oh, my God, dude, I got out of there like I had a fucking map.'
jokes.setup: Jonah insults Dan and Amy about their births; Dan responds
jokes.punchline: Dan's counter — 'I had a fucking map' — is both a direct boast about his birth (he was confident/prepared even then) and a brilliantly absurd image
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes. The image of baby Dan emerging from the womb with a map — prepared, navigating, already in control — is perfectly character-integrated. It's exactly how Dan would conceive of himself: competent even at birth. The 'I had a fucking map' is immediately quotable and the confidence with which it's delivered makes it even funnier. High marks across the board — originality because the specific image is fresh, character integration because only Dan would say this.

jokes.text: 'Dan, get out of my car.' / 'Do you think I want to be in this fucking Minecraft piece of shit?'
jokes.setup: Dan has gotten into Jonah's car; Jonah wants him out
jokes.punchline: 'Minecraft piece of shit' as a description of Jonah's car suggests it's blocky, cheap, and ugly — a gaming insult applied to real-world transportation
jokes.explanation: The Minecraft insult for a car is unexpectedly effective — it conjures a pixelated, poorly designed rectangle. Dan doesn't want to be there but can't help himself insulting Jonah's vehicle even as he sits in it. Good character moment for Dan.

jokes.text: 'You colossal fucking fanny pack. You don't get it, do you? They only sent you 'cause they knew that you would fuck it up. You're so stupid, you don't even know that you're being used for your stupidity.'
jokes.setup: Dan is trapped in Jonah's car and trying to humiliate him
jokes.punchline: The escalating insult culminating in 'so stupid you don't know you're being used for your stupidity' is a logical paradox of incompetence — the ultimate catch-22 of being too dumb to know you're the designated idiot
jokes.explanation: 'You're so stupid, you don't even know that you're being used for your stupidity' is a genuinely clever construction — it's a self-referential logical trap for idiocy. The escalation from 'fanny pack' to this philosophical statement about meta-stupidity is very well-structured. 'Colossal fucking fanny pack' deserves mention as an absurdist insult — a fanny pack is already slightly embarrassing, making it 'colossal' and 'fucking' creates the right scale of contempt. Meets the 8.0 craft anchor.

jokes.text: Jonah's car won't start; he reverses as a fix; Dan points out he should just go straight — 'I took a precision driving class, Dan. I think I know what I'm doing.'
jokes.setup: After the hospital standoff, they need to chase Pierce; Jonah's car has a glitch
jokes.punchline: Jonah responding to 'just go straight' by reversing, then claiming 'precision driving class' credentials is perfect Jonah — incompetent but smugly confident
jokes.explanation: Jonah claiming a 'precision driving class' while demonstrably not knowing which direction to go is good character comedy. The specificity of 'precision driving class' as Jonah's claimed expertise is funny — it's not a common credential to cite.

jokes.text: 'We should have hired Navy SEALs instead of those two actual seals.'
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy have lost Pierce; Selina is furious
jokes.punchline: 'Those two actual seals' — implying Dan and Amy are the marine mammal kind, not the elite military kind — is a clean, well-constructed pun delivered with perfect contempt
jokes.explanation: This is excellent Veep writing. The setup is in the first clause (Navy SEALs) and the punchline redefines the referent (those two actual seals = Dan and Amy are animals). Perfect economy — eleven words, complete joke, devastating character assessment. The wordplay is clean and the contempt is real. Among the episode's best one-liners.

jokes.text: 'How do you lose a whole person? A cuff link I get, but not a person.'
jokes.setup: Pierce has been lost by the team
jokes.punchline: The comparison item — 'a cuff link I get' — implies Selina routinely loses cuff links and finds that completely understandable, making the person benchmark funny
jokes.explanation: The 'cuff link' specificity is what makes this. Not 'keys' or 'wallet' — cuff links, which are both small and class-specific. The implication that Selina frequently misplaces cuff links but draws the line at losing people is absurd and very funny.

jokes.text: Selina's insult speech: 'You're parasites. You're like an infestation of mediocrity. I don't care if you're a 1950s radio broadcaster. You're Fozzie Bear who's been ripped up and used to smuggle heroin. A Nazi doctor. I don't even know what the fuck you are.'
jokes.setup: Selina has snapped and is dressing down her entire team for the failure
jokes.punchline: The insult sequence escalates from coherent (parasites, mediocrity) to cultural reference (1950s radio broadcaster) to deeply specific absurdism (Fozzie Bear smuggling heroin) to complete confusion ('I don't even know what the fuck you are')
jokes.explanation: This rant is one of the episode's standout moments. What elevates it beyond a normal insult speech is the escalating disconnection from sense — it starts with political insults ('infestation of mediocrity') then veers into the completely surreal. 'Fozzie Bear who's been ripped up and used to smuggle heroin' is a genuinely inspired absurdist image — it's specific enough to be visualized, weird enough to be funny, and the additional detail of heroin smuggling takes it somewhere no normal insult would go. The finale — 'I don't even know what the fuck you are' — admits that she's lost her own thread, which is the perfect anti-climax. Very high originality score because this sequence is unlike anything else in the episode or most television.

jokes.text: 'Call NASA. They've got to have a satellite that can pinpoint a moron.'
jokes.setup: Selina orders the team to find Pierce
jokes.punchline: Invoking NASA as moron-detection technology is both absurd and politically apt — Selina actually has access to government satellites
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Selina actually COULD call NASA — she's the president. The application of presidential power to detecting a 'moron' is perfectly character-integrated. The idea that NASA satellites could 'pinpoint a moron' is both absurd and strangely logical given Pierce's profile. Excellent line.

jokes.text: Gary trying to end the team talk: 'Tom. Tom.' / 'Shut up, Gary.' / 'Shut... mm-hmm.' / 'I do the team talks, okay?'
jokes.setup: Selina has just given a rousing speech; Tom echoes her
jokes.punchline: Gary's attempt to contribute by saying 'Tom. Tom.' — presumably to echo enthusiasm — gets immediately shot down, and his meek 'Shut... mm-hmm' is the perfect Gary capitulation
jokes.explanation: 'Shut... mm-hmm' is Gary perfectly compressed — he starts to push back, immediately surrenders, and then agrees she's right. Pure Gary in three words.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Okay, new rule in the Cube. If your name begins with D, you need to shut the fuck up immediately.' Richard: 'I'm fine because my name begins with an R.' Dan: 'Not if we shorten it to Dick.'
jokes.setup: Dan imposes a rule to silence himself and Jonah (whose names begin with D... wait, Jonah begins with J) — but the rule catches Richard
jokes.punchline: Richard's innocent relief ('my name begins with R!') immediately followed by Dan's 'not if we shorten it to Dick' is a perfect trap-spring
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured — the arbitrary rule, Richard's relief, the immediate loophole. 'Not if we shorten it to Dick' is Dan at his most casually cruel. Richard's innocent logic 'my name begins with R' makes his vulnerability funnier. High structure, excellent economy, strong character integration. One of the episode's best constructed jokes.

jokes.text: Pierce explaining why he left his sick mother alone: 'Guys, my mom is too ill to be left alone right now. I mean, I left her alone right now because she likes to read. But she can't read right now because she's very sick.'
jokes.setup: Pierce is trying to justify abandoning his sick mother to be lobbied
jokes.punchline: The logic spirals: she's too ill to be alone, I left her alone because she reads, she can't read because she's sick — each clause undoes the previous one
jokes.explanation: Pierce's circular logic is genuinely well constructed — each sentence contradicts the previous one in a way that reveals a character who is both dim and trying his best. The structure is tight: thesis → exception → exception to the exception → full circle back to the thesis. Good absurdist escalation.

jokes.text: Temperature disagreement: 'I'm cold.' / 'It's actually about 110 in here.' / 'You could grow tomatoes in here.'
jokes.setup: Selina has a fever; the room apparently is very warm because of her
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'cold/hot' to 'you could grow tomatoes in here' — a horticultural level of heat that implies the president's fever is greenhouse-level
jokes.explanation: 'You could grow tomatoes in here' is a decent extension of the heat joke. Functional, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Amy and Dan stopped at the White House door: 'You can't come in. That comes from the president.' Amy: 'Well, she's a bitch.' Jonah: 'I'll tell her you said hi.'
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan are blocked from entering the White House after doing the work
jokes.punchline: Amy's petulant 'she's a bitch' followed by Jonah's perfectly chipper 'I'll tell her you said hi' — Jonah genuinely helpful in the most aggravating way possible
jokes.explanation: 'I'll tell her you said hi' is a great Jonah line — he's too dim to realize he shouldn't offer this, and his helpfulness is the cruelest thing he could do. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Richard insisting on introducing himself to the president: 'I brought him also, ma'am. Richard Splett.' / 'She already knows who you are. You can go.' / 'No, no, no. Not you. Not you.'
jokes.setup: Jonah introduces Pierce; Richard tries to take credit too
jokes.punchline: The confusion over who's being dismissed — Richard told to go, then Jonah corrected — and Richard's earnest need to be acknowledged is a sustained cringe beat
jokes.explanation: Good Richard character beat — his need to be seen and acknowledged, even by the president, is touching and pathetic simultaneously.

jokes.text: Pierce negotiating his ambassadorship: 'What's your favorite city?' / 'Dallas. I love Dallas, Texas.' / 'No, it needs to be a foreign city.'
jokes.setup: Pierce is being offered an ambassadorship in exchange for his vote
jokes.punchline: Pierce's 'Dallas. I love Dallas, Texas' — suggesting a domestic city for a foreign ambassadorship — reveals his total ignorance of what an ambassador does
jokes.explanation: Excellently constructed character joke. 'Dallas. I love Dallas, Texas.' lands because it's specific (he even says 'Texas' as if clarifying which Dallas) and reveals total political/diplomatic incompetence. The correction 'No, it needs to be a foreign city' is the straight-faced punchline. Could only be Pierce.

jokes.text: 'There's Pierce. He even walks dumb. Every time I see him, I hear circus music.'
jokes.setup: Pierce appears on screen to vote
jokes.punchline: 'He even walks dumb' is a vivid and mean observation; 'every time I see him, I hear circus music' extends it with an auditory dimension that's both creative and cruel
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the 'elf' characterization. 'He even walks dumb' — not just 'walks stupidly' but 'walks dumb,' treating stupidity as a physical gait — is original. The circus music extension gives it an extra creative layer.

tmdb_overview: Gary sets up a secret meeting with Dan and Amy to persuade them to lobby against Selina's bill.
insight: Veep hits 88 with 99 jokes in 38 minutes—peak output density across the entire series.
